html:
[1011]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有殊色 - Means 'unusually beautiful' or 'extraordinarily attractive,' used to describe physical appearance.
  • 常與計事 - Means 'often involved in planning or decision-making,' indicating a role in advisory or strategic matters.
  • 傾都邑 - Means 'to sway or influence the entire city,' suggesting widespread impact or influence.
  • 依倚 - Means 'to rely on' or 'to depend on,' often with a connotation of dependence or favoritism.
  • 調笑 - Means 'to tease' or 'to jest,' often in a playful or flirtatious manner.
  • 廣治第室 - Means 'to extensively build or renovate one's mansion,' indicating lavish construction or decoration.
  • 作乘舆辇 - Means 'to make a carriage fit for an emperor,' suggesting the creation of a grand or imperial vehicle.
  • 加画绣 - Means 'to add embroidery or painting,' indicating decorative embellishment.
  • 黄金涂韦絮荐轮 - Means 'to coat the wheels with gold and use soft padding,' describing luxurious vehicle construction.
  • 挽显及子都 - Means 'to pull or drag the princess and her lover,' suggesting a playful or symbolic act.
  • 天子娶妇，皇后嫁女 - Means 'the emperor's wedding and the empress's daughter's marriage,' used metaphorically to describe grand events.
  • 大官私官，并供其第 - Means 'officials from both the imperial and private sectors provided for the mansion,' indicating widespread support or service.
  • 两宫使者，冠盖不绝 - Means 'envoys from both the imperial and empress's palaces kept arriving without interruption,' suggesting constant attention or favor.
  • 赏赐以千万数 - Means 'rewards were given in the millions,' indicating lavish or frequent rewards.
  • 得幸上 - Means 'to gain the emperor's favor,' indicating a close or favored relationship.
  • 卧起 - Means 'to sleep and rise,' indicating a close or intimate relationship.
  • 微行出游 - Means 'to go out in disguise,' suggesting secretive or covert outings.
  • 北至甘泉，南至长阳、五 - Means 'traveling north to Ganquan and south to Changyang and Wu,' indicating specific locations.
  • 斗鸡走马 - Means 'to fight cocks and ride horses,' suggesting leisure activities.
  • 长安中 - Means 'in the capital Chang'an,' indicating the setting of the activities.
  • 积数年 - Means 'for many years,' indicating a long duration.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大将军霍光监奴冯子都 - Refers to Feng Zidu, a slave of General-in-Chief Huo Guang, a powerful figure in the Western Han dynasty.
  • 霍家奴 - Refers to a slave of the Huo family, indicating a person of low status but with significant influence.
  • 依倚将军势 - Refers to relying on the power of a general, suggesting a relationship of dependence or favoritism.
  • 酒家胡 - Refers to a foreigner (often from Central Asia) working in a tavern, suggesting a person of a different ethnicity or social status.
  • 富平侯张放 - Refers to Zhang Fang, a nobleman during the Western Han dynasty, known for his close relationship with Emperor Cheng.
  • 大司马安世曾孙 - Refers to Zhang Fang as the great-grandson of Zhang Anshi, a prominent official during the Western Han dynasty.
  • 敬武公主 - Refers to Princess Jingwu, a daughter of Emperor Cheng.
  • 成帝 - Refers to Emperor Cheng, the ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 遵武帝故事 - Refers to following the precedents set by Emperor Wu, indicating a desire to emulate a past ruler.
  • 近臣游宴 - Refers to outings with close officials, suggesting leisure activities with trusted advisors.
  • 侍中中郎将 - Refers to a high-ranking official position, indicating a prestigious role.
  • 监平乐屯兵 - Refers to overseeing the troops stationed at Pingle, suggesting a military role.
  • 幕府 - Refers to a military office or headquarters, indicating a place of authority.
  • 仪比将军 - Refers to having a status or protocol similar to that of a general, indicating a high level of respect or authority.
  • 卧起 - Refers to sleeping and rising together, indicating a close or intimate relationship.
  • 宠爱殊绝 - Refers to extraordinary favor or affection, suggesting a unique or exceptional relationship.
  • 微行出游 - Refers to going out in disguise, suggesting secretive or covert outings.
  • 甘泉 - Refers to Ganquan, a palace or location in the Western Han dynasty.
  • 长阳、五 - Refers to Changyang and Wu, specific locations in the Western Han dynasty.
  • 斗鸡走马 - Refers to leisure activities such as cockfighting and horse racing, indicating pastimes enjoyed by the elite.
  • 长安中 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital city of the Western Han dynasty, indicating the setting of the activities.

Proper Nouns

  • 大将军霍光 - General-in-Chief Huo Guang, a powerful figure in the Western Han dynasty.
  • 冯子都 - Feng Zidu, a slave of Huo Guang.
  • 光 - Huo Guang, the general-in-chief.
  • 显 - Princess Huo, the widow of Huo Guang.
  • 富平侯张放 - Zhang Fang, a nobleman during the Western Han dynasty.
  • 大司马安世曾孙 - Zhang Fang, the great-grandson of Zhang Anshi.
  • 敬武公主 - Princess Jingwu, a daughter of Emperor Cheng.

[1012]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是时 - Means 'at that time' or 'in those days,' setting the temporal context.
  • 上诸舅皆害其宠 - Means 'the emperor's uncles all resented his favor,' indicating jealousy or envy.
  • 白太后 - Means 'reported to the empress dowager,' showing the action of informing.
  • 以上春秋富 - Means 'because of the emperor's youth,' where '春秋' is a euphemism for age, often used to refer to young men.
  • 动作不节 - Means 'his actions were not restrained,' indicating lack of discipline.
  • 甚以过放 - Means 'they greatly blamed him,' where '过放' suggests excessive leniency or indulgence.
  • 议者过咎放等 - Means 'those who debated blamed him and others,' indicating criticism.
  • 左迁 - Means 'demoted' or 'transferred to a lower position,' a euphemism for demotion.
  • 望书劳问不绝 - Means 'continued to send letters of concern and inquiry,' showing ongoing communication.
  • 望书 - Means 'letters of concern,' indicating expressions of worry or care.
  • 劳问 - Means 'inquiries and concerns,' indicating expressions of worry or care.
  • 征放归第 - Means 'recalled him to his home,' indicating a return to his residence.
  • 视母公主疾 - Means 'to attend to his mother, the princess,' indicating caring for her health.
  • 比年日蚀 - Means 'repeated solar eclipses,' indicating frequent natural phenomena.
  • 永始、元延间 - Refers to the years of Yongshi and Yuanyan, specific periods in the reign of Emperor Cheng.
  • 故久不还 - Means 'therefore, he remained away for a long time,' indicating a prolonged absence.
  • 征放归第 - Means 'recalled him to his home,' indicating a return to his residence.
  • 视母公主疾 - Means 'to attend to his mother, the princess,' indicating caring for her health.
  • 数月，主有廖 - Means 'after several months, the princess recovered,' indicating improvement in her health.
  • 出放为河东都尉 - Means 'sent him out as the commander of Hedong,' indicating a change in his administrative role.
  • 涕泣而遣之 - Means 'shed tears while sending him off,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 复征为侍中光禄大夫 - Means 'recalled him again as the Grand Chamberlain and Grand Master,' indicating a higher rank.
  • 秩中二千石 - Means 'ranked at the level of 2,000 stones,' indicating a high official rank.
  • 岁余 - Means 'after a year or so,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 故常涕泣而遣之 - Means 'therefore, he often shed tears while sending him off,' indicating repeated emotional distress.
  • 后复征为侍中光禄大夫 - Means 'later, he was recalled again as the Grand Chamberlain and Grand Master,' indicating a repeated recall.
  • 秩中二千石 - Means 'ranked at the level of 2,000 stones,' indicating a high official rank.
  • 岁余 - Means 'after a year or so,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 丞相方进复奏放 - Means 'the chancellor Fang Jin again reported against him,' indicating another accusation.
  • 上不得已免放 - Means 'the emperor had no choice but to dismiss him,' indicating a forced decision.
  • 赐钱五百万 - Means 'gave him five million coins,' indicating a financial reward.
  • 遣就国 - Means 'sent him to his fief,' indicating a demotion or exile.
  • 数月，成帝崩 - Means 'after several months, Emperor Cheng died,' indicating a significant event.
  • 放思慕哭泣而死 - Means 'Fang mourned and wept until he died,' indicating his deep grief.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 上诸舅 - Refers to the emperor's uncles, who were powerful figures in the court.
  • 太后 - Refers to the empress dowager, a powerful figure in the imperial family.
  • 成帝 - Refers to Emperor Cheng, the ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 永始、元延 - Specific years in the reign of Emperor Cheng, indicating a particular period.
  • 天水 - Refers to Tianshui, a region in modern-day Gansu province.
  • 河东 - Refers to Hedong, a region in modern-day Shanxi province.
  • 侍中 - A high-ranking court official, often involved in advising the emperor.
  • 光禄大夫 - A high-ranking official, often involved in ceremonial and advisory roles.
  • 中二千石 - A high official rank, indicating a position of significant importance.
  • 丞相 - Refers to the chancellor, the highest-ranking official in the government.
  • 方进 - Refers to Fang Jin, a prominent official who opposed Fang.

Proper Nouns

  • 上 - The emperor.
  • 诸舅 - The emperor's uncles.
  • 太后 - The empress dowager.
  • 成帝 - Emperor Cheng.
  • 永始、元延 - Specific years in the reign of Emperor Cheng.
  • 天水 - Tianshui, a region in modern-day Gansu province.
  • 河东 - Hedong, a region in modern-day Shanxi province.
  • 侍中 - A high-ranking court official.
  • 光禄大夫 - A high-ranking official.
  • 中二千石 - A high official rank.
  • 丞相 - The chancellor.
  • 方进 - Fang Jin, a prominent official.
  • 放 - Fang, the subject of the passage.

[1013]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 為御史 - Government official, specifically an inspector or censor.
  • 任賢為太子舍人 - Appointed as an attendant of the crown prince.
  • 出則參乘 - Ride beside the emperor, a sign of favor.
  • 入御左右 - Serve at the emperor's side.
  • 旬月間 - A month or so.
  • 賞賜累矩萬 - Reward accumulated to ten thousand units of currency.
  • 偏藉上袖 - Rest on one side of the emperor's sleeve.
  • 乃斷袖而起 - Cut the sleeve and rise, a metaphor for extreme favor.
  • 性柔和便辟 - Nature is gentle and flattering.
  • 善為媚以自固 - Good at flattery to secure oneself.
  • 每賜洗沐不肯出 - Refused to leave each time granted leave for rest.
  • 常留中視醫藥 - Often stay in the palace to attend to medical treatment.
  • 召賢女弟以為昭儀 - Summoned the younger sister of Gong Xian to appoint as Dowager Consort.
  • 位次皇后 - Ranked after the empress.
  • 更名其舍為椒風 - Renamed her residence as Pepper Wind.
  • 以配椒房雲 - To match the spice room (a euphemism for the empress's quarters).
  • 賞賜昭儀及賢妻，亦各千萬數 - Rewarded Dowager Consort and wife of Gong Xian, each ten thousand.
  • 遷賢父為少府 - Promoted the father of Gong Xian to Minister of the Imperial Household.
  • 賜爵關內侯，食邑 - Bestowed the title of Marquis within the Pass and granted a fief.
  • 復徙為衛尉 - Transferred again to Commander of the Palace Guards.
  • 又以賢妻父為將作大匠 - Also appointed the father-in-law of Gong Xian as Master of Works.
  • 弟為執金吾 - Younger brother appointed as Commander of the Capital Garrison.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 董賢 - Gong Xian, a favorite of Emperor Ai of Han, known for his beauty and favoritism.
  • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han, who ruled from 7 BC to 1 BC.
  • 太子舍人 - An attendant of the crown prince, indicating a position of trust.
  • 黃門郎 - An official in the palace, often responsible for attending to the emperor.
  • 光祿大夫 - A high-ranking official in the imperial household.
  • 昭儀 - A high-ranking imperial consort, second only to the empress.
  • 椒房 - A euphemism for the empress's quarters, often associated with the empress's residence.
  • 少府 - A high-ranking official responsible for the imperial household.
  • 關內侯 - A noble title, indicating a high rank within the imperial hierarchy.
  • 衛尉 - A high-ranking official responsible for the security of the palace.
  • 將作大匠 - An official responsible for construction and public works.
  • 執金吾 - An official responsible for maintaining order in the capital.

[1014]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 诏将作大匠为贤起大第北阙下 - Imperial decree to the Grand Master of Works to build a grand mansion for the nobleman '贤' near the northern gate of the imperial palace.
  • 重殿洞门，土木之功，穷极技巧 - Description of the grandeur of the palace with multiple halls and open doors, and the construction work that exhausted all possible techniques.
  • 柱槛衣以纬锦 - Columns and railings adorned with brocade, indicating luxurious decoration.
  • 武库禁兵，上方珍宝 - Elite imperial guards and treasures of the imperial treasury.
  • 选物上弟 - Selected items of the highest quality.
  • 乘舆服乃其副也 - Imperial carriage and clothing were subordinate to these treasures.
  • 东园秘器，珠襦玉押 - Secret imperial workshops and imperial treasures, including jeweled robes and jade ornaments.
  • 益封贤二千户 - Additional fiefdom of 2,000 households granted to the nobleman '贤'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 诏 - Imperial decree, indicating the authority of the emperor.
  • 将作大匠 - High-ranking official responsible for imperial construction projects.
  • 北阙 - Northern gate of the imperial palace, symbolizing the center of imperial power.
  • 武库 - Armory, indicating the storage of military equipment.
  • 上方 - Imperial treasury, symbolizing the wealth and resources of the empire.
  • 东园 - Secret imperial workshops, where precious items were crafted.
  • 侯 - Noble title, indicating the highest rank of nobility in the Han dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 贤 - Nobleman who was granted a grand mansion and other honors.
  • 董氏 - Family name of the nobleman who was granted a grand mansion and other honors.

[1015]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 內疑 - Means 'doubting internally' or 'suspiciously'; indicates that the Chancellor Wang Jia had doubts about the East Ping case.
  • 惡躬等 - Means 'hated Gao and others'; refers to Chancellor Wang Jia's dislike for Gao and his associates.
  • 以賢為亂國制度 - Means 'considered Xian as disrupting the state's system'; where '賢' refers to Dong Xian, and '亂國制度' means disrupting the state's system.
  • 嘉竟坐言事下獄死 - Means 'Jia was ultimately punished for speaking out and died in prison'; where '坐言事' means 'punished for speaking out.'
  • 上初即位 - Means 'the emperor just ascended the throne'; indicates the beginning of the emperor's reign.
  • 兩家先貴 - Means 'both families were previously honored'; indicates the high status of the two families.
  • 傅太后從弟喜 - Means 'the younger brother of Empress Dowager Fu, Xi'; indicates a familial relationship.
  • 數諫，失太后指 - Means 'frequently advised, but missed the Empress Dowager's intentions'; indicates that Xi's advice was not well-received.
  • 明甚憐之 - Means 'Ming deeply pitied him'; indicates the emperor's sympathy for Xi.
  • 遂冊免明 - Means 'thus, the emperor issued a decree to dismiss Ming'; indicates the formal removal of Ming from his position.
  • 前東平王雲 - Means 'the former Prince of Dongping, Yun'; indicates a historical figure.
  • 詞祭祝詛 - Means 'used words in sacrificial rituals to curse'; indicates a form of ritualistic cursing.
  • 後舅伍宏 - Means 'the husband of the princess, Wu Hong'; indicates a familial relationship.
  • 以醫待詔 - Means 'served as an imperial physician'; indicates a professional role.
  • 與校祕書郎楊罔結謀反 - Means 'conspired with the Imperial Secretariat clerk Yang Wang to rebel'; indicates a plot to overthrow the government.
  • 逆禍迫切 - Means 'the disaster was imminent'; indicates the urgency of the situation.
  • 賴宗廟神靈，董賢等以聞 - Means 'thanks to the divine protection of the ancestors, Dong Xian and others reported it'; indicates the role of divine intervention and the actions of Dong Xian.
  • 咸伏其辜 - Means 'all were punished for their crimes'; indicates the punishment of those involved.
  • 從弟侍中奉車都尉吳族 - Means 'the younger brother of the Prince of Dongping, Wu Zong, who served as an attendant in the carriage'; indicates a familial and professional relationship.
  • 父左曹屯騎校尉宣 - Means 'the father, Xu, who served as the Left Cao and Cavalry Commandant'; indicates a professional role.
  • 皆知宏及栩、丹諸侯王後親 - Means 'all knew Hong and Xu, the sons-in-law of the princes'; indicates a network of relationships.
  • 宣除用丹為御屬 - Means 'Xu appointed Dan as an attendant'; indicates a professional action.
  • 吳與宏交通，厚善數稱薦宏 - Means 'Wu and Hong had close ties, and Wu frequently recommended Hong'; indicates a personal and professional relationship.
  • 宏以附吳，興其惡心 - Means 'Hong relied on Wu and harbored evil intentions'; indicates a negative relationship.
  • 因醫技進，幾危社稷 - Means 'through his medical skills, he nearly endangered the state'; indicates the potential threat posed by Hong.
  • 朕以恭皇后故不忍有雲 - Means 'I, due to the respect for Empress Gong, cannot speak of this'; indicates the emperor's reluctance to criticize Hong due to family ties.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 丞相王嘉 - Refers to Chancellor Wang Jia, a high-ranking official during the Western Han dynasty.
  • 東平王雲 - Refers to the former Prince of Dongping, Yun, a historical figure.
  • 大司马辅政 - Refers to the position of Grand Marshal, a high-ranking military and political office.
  • 董賢 - Refers to Dong Xian, a trusted advisor to the emperor.
  • 伍宏 - Refers to Wu Hong, a historical figure who served as an imperial physician.
  • 楊罔 - Refers to Yang Wang, a historical figure who served as an Imperial Secretariat clerk.
  • 恭皇后 - Refers to Empress Gong, the emperor's mother, who held significant influence.

Proper Nouns

  • 丞相王嘉 - Chancellor Wang Jia
  • 東平王雲 - Former Prince of Dongping, Yun
  • 傅太后 - Empress Dowager Fu
  • 丁太后 - Empress Dowager Ding
  • 大司马辅政 - Grand Marshal, a high-ranking military and political office
  • 董賢 - Dong Xian
  • 伍宏 - Wu Hong
  • 楊罔 - Yang Wang
  • 恭皇后 - Empress Gong

[1016]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 位尊任重 - Formal expression meaning 'holding a high position with great responsibilities.'
  • 明威立义 - Means 'to establish clear authority and righteousness.'
  • 折消未萌 - Means 'to eliminate potential threats before they arise.'
  • 怀非君上 - Means 'to harbor thoughts that are not in favor of the ruler.'
  • 阿为宣、吴反痛恨云等 - Complex phrase that can be interpreted as 'supporting the actions of Xuan and Wu while harboring resentment towards Yun and others.'
  • 亲见言伍宏善医，死可惜也 - Means 'personally stating that Wu Hong is a good doctor, and it is a pity if he dies.'
  • 贤等获封极幸 - Means 'it is extremely fortunate for the virtuous ones to be granted titles.'
  • 嫉妒忠良，非毁有功 - Means 'envying the loyal and virtuous, and slandering those who have achieved merit.'
  • 于戏伤哉 - Lamentation meaning 'alas, how sad!'
  • 君亲无将，将而诛之 - Principle meaning 'there is no rebellion against one's ruler or parent; if there is, it must be punished.'
  • 季友鸩叔牙 - Refers to a historical event where Jizi (季友) poisoned Shuyi (叔牙) to prevent a coup.
  • 赵盾不讨贼，谓之弑君 - Refers to a historical event where Zhao Dun (赵盾) failed to punish the assassins of his ruler, leading to his being called a regicide.
  • 噬肤之恩未忍 - Means 'the kindness of being treated like family is still remembered and unwilling to punish.'
  • 上骠骑将军印绶，罢归就第 - Means 'to remove the rank of Grand Marshal of Cavalry, and to retire to one's home.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 将军 - Refers to a high-ranking military officer.
  • 宣、吴 - Likely refers to specific individuals or factions within the court.
  • 云、宏 - Refers to specific individuals, likely court officials or advisors.
  • 季友 - A historical figure known for his role in the State of Lu during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 叔牙 - A historical figure who was poisoned by Jizi (季友).
  • 赵盾 - A historical figure known for his role in the State of Jin during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 噬肤之恩 - A metaphor for the deep familial bond and kindness.
  • 骠骑将军 - A high-ranking military title in the Han dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 将军 - A high-ranking military officer.
  • 宣、吴 - Specific individuals or factions within the court.
  • 云、宏 - Specific individuals, likely court officials or advisors.
  • 季友 - A historical figure known for his role in the State of Lu.
  • 叔牙 - A historical figure who was poisoned by Jizi (季友).
  • 赵盾 - A historical figure known for his role in the State of Jin.
  • 丞相嘉 - A specific individual, likely a high-ranking official.
  • 骠骑将军 - A high-ranking military title in the Han dynasty.

[1017]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雖為三公，常給事中領尚書 - Although holding the position of Grand Councillor, he often served as an attendant in the palace and oversaw the Shangshu (Chancellery).
  • 百官因賢奏事 - All officials submitted their reports through him.
  • 徙為光禄大夫，秩中二千石 - He was transferred to the position of Grand Officer of the Palace, with a rank equivalent to 2,000 measures of grain.
  • 弟宽信代賢為驸馬都尉 - His younger brother Kuanxin succeeded him as the Imperial Son-in-Law.
  • 董氏親屬皆侍中諸曹奉朝請，寵在丁、傅之右矣 - Members of the Dong family were all appointed as Attendants and Ministers, receiving regular invitations to court, and their favor surpassed that of the Ding and Fu families.
  • 宴見，群臣在前，單于怪賢年少 - During a banquet, with all officials present, the Chanyu was surprised by the young age of Dong.
  • 以問譯 - He asked the interpreter.
  • 上令譯報曰 - The emperor instructed the interpreter to reply.
  • 大司馬年少，以大賢居位 - The Grand Marshal is young, but he holds a position due to his great virtue.
  • 乃起，拜賀漢朝得賢臣 - He then stood up and bowed, congratulating the Han court on having a virtuous minister.
  • 初，丞相孔光為御史大夫，時賢父恭為御史，事光 - Initially, Chancellor Kong Guang was the Grand Censor, and at that time, Dong's father Gong was also a Censor, serving under Kong Guang.
  • 及賢為大司馬，與光並為三公 - When Dong became the Grand Marshal, he and Kong Guang were both among the Three Excellencies.
  • 上故令賢私過光 - The emperor intentionally had Dong visit Kong Guang privately.
  • 光雅恭謹，知上欲尊寵賢 - Kong Guang, being naturally respectful and cautious, understood that the emperor intended to honor Dong.
  • 及聞賢當來也，光警戒衣冠，出門待望 - When he heard that Dong was coming, Kong Guang dressed formally and waited outside.
  • 見賢車，乃卻入 - Seeing Dong's carriage, he retreated inside.
  • 賢至中門，光入閣 - When Dong reached the middle gate, Kong Guang entered the hall.
  • 既下車，乃出拜謁 - After Dong had alighted from his carriage, he stepped out to pay respects.
  • 送迎甚謹，不敢以賓客均敵之禮 - They received and saw him off with great respect,不敢 using the same courteous treatment as for equals.
  • 賢歸，上聞之喜，立拜光兩兄子為大夫、常侍 - When Dong returned, the emperor, upon hearing about it, was pleased and immediately appointed Kong Guang's two nephews as Attendants and Regular Attendants.
  • 賢由是權與人主侔矣 - From then on, Dong's power was equal to that of the emperor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三公 - Refers to the highest-ranking officials in the Han government, typically including the Chancellor, the Grand Marshal, and the Grand Commandant.
  • 給事中 - A position in the imperial court that involved advising the emperor and overseeing various administrative tasks.
  • 尚書 - The Shangshu (Chancellery) was a central government institution responsible for drafting and issuing imperial edicts.
  • 光祿大夫 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court, often involved in ceremonial duties.
  • 駙馬都尉 - A position held by the emperor's sons-in-law, often involving military and administrative responsibilities.
  • 侍中 - A position in the imperial court that involved serving as an attendant to the emperor and participating in important state affairs.
  • 諸曹 - Refers to various departments or offices within the imperial administration.
  • 奉朝請 - A regular invitation to attend court, often a sign of favor and importance.
  • 單于 - The title of the leader of the Xiongnu, a nomadic confederation that frequently interacted with the Han dynasty.
  • 大司馬 - The highest military rank, equivalent to a grand marshal.
  • 丞相 - The highest civilian official, equivalent to a prime minister.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 禁止賢不得入宫殿司马中 - Prohibit the Virtuous One from entering the palace as Grand Marshal
  • 賢不知所为，诣闭免冠徒跣谢 - The Virtuous One did not know what to do, went to the closed gate, removed his hat, and stood barefoot in apology
  • 莽使谒者以太后诏，即阙下册贤曰 - Wang Mang sent a court official to deliver the Empress Dowager's edict, and at the palace gate, he formally demoted the Virtuous One, saying
  • 间者以来，阴阳不调，灾害并臻，元元蒙辜 - Since then, the yin and yang have been out of balance, disasters have occurred simultaneously, and the common people have suffered
  • 高安侯贤未更事理，为大司马，不合众心，非所以折冲绥远也 - The Marquis of Gao'an, the Virtuous One, has not experienced state affairs, serving as Grand Marshal, and is not in harmony with the people's hearts; this is not how to repel the enemy and pacify the distant
  • 其收司马印绶，罢归第 - Seize the seal and sash of the Grand Marshal, and dismiss him from his post, returning him to his residence
  • 即日，贤与妻皆自杀，家惶恐夜葬 - On that day, the Virtuous One and his wife both committed suicide, and their family hastily buried them at night
  • 莽疑其诈，有司奏请发贤棺至狱诊视 - Wang Mang suspected it was a fraud, and the officials proposed to open the Virtuous One's coffin at the prison for examination
  • 莽复讽大司徒光奏 - Wang Mang then indirectly instructed the Grand Tutor Guang to submit a report
  • 贤质性巧佞，翼好以获封侯 - The Virtuous One's nature is cunning and sycophantic, and he gained his title as Marquis through flattery
  • 父子专朝，兄弟并宠 - Father and son monopolized the court, and brothers shared favor
  • 多受赐，治第宅，造家扩，放效无极，不异玉制，费以万万计，国家为空虚 - He received many gifts, built mansions, constructed family estates, imitated extravagance without limit, differing from the imperial standards, spending millions of units, leaving the state impoverished
  • 父子骄麦，至不为使者礼，受赐不拜，罪恶暴著 - Father and son were arrogant, to the point of not showing respect to envoys, not bowing when receiving gifts, with their crimes and vices openly evident

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 禁止賢不得入宫殿司马中 - This phrase is elliptical, implying that Wang Xun was prevented from entering the palace in his role as Grand Marshal.
  • 賢不知所为，诣闭免冠徒跣谢 - This sequence describes Wang Xun's actions in response to the prohibition, but the exact order of events is not clear.
  • 莽使谒者以太后诏，即阙下册贤曰 - This clause is elliptical, omitting the specific content of the edict.
  • 间者以来，阴阳不调，灾害并臻，元元蒙辜 - This sequence lists a series of calamities, but the exact relationship between them is not specified.
  • 高安侯贤未更事理，为大司马，不合众心，非所以折冲绥远也 - This clause is a formal critique of Wang Xun's suitability for the position of Grand Marshal.
  • 其收司马印绶，罢归第 - This clause is elliptical, omitting the specific details of the demotion.
  • 即日，贤与妻皆自杀，家惶恐夜葬 - This sequence describes the immediate aftermath of the demotion, but the exact timing and circumstances are not clear.
  • 莽疑其诈，有司奏请发贤棺至狱诊视 - This clause is elliptical, omitting the specific reasons for the examination.
  • 莽复讽大司徒光奏 - This clause is elliptical, omitting the specific content of the report.
  • 贤质性巧佞，翼好以获封侯 - This clause is a formal critique of Wang Xun's character and rise to power.
  • 父子专朝，兄弟并宠 - This clause is a formal critique of the Wang family's monopolization of power.
  • 多受赐，治第宅，造家扩，放效无极，不异玉制，费以万万计，国家为空虚 - This clause is a formal critique of the Wang family's extravagance and its impact on the state.
  • 父子骄麦，至不为使者礼，受赐不拜，罪恶暴著 - This clause is a formal critique of the Wang family's arrogance and disrespect.

  • 莽 - Wang Mang, the regent and eventual usurper of the Han dynasty
  • 太后 - Empress Dowager Wang, Wang Mang's aunt

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尚淫奢 - Indulging in luxury and extravagance, describing the emperor's lifestyle.
  • 多進谄佞幸爱之臣 - Frequently admitting flatterers, sycophants, and favored officials, indicating the emperor's preference for sycophantic courtiers.
  • 竟以妆饰妖丽，巧言取容 - Ultimately using elaborate and alluring adornments, and clever words to win favor, describing how these officials gained the emperor's trust.
  • 董贤以雾绡革衣，飘若蝉翼 - Fine gauze and leather garments, light as a cicada's wing, highlighting Dong Xian's elegance and grace.
  • 帝入燕息之房，命筵卿易轻衣小袖，不用奢带修裙，故使宛转便易也 - The emperor's command to Dong Xian to change into lighter clothing and shorter sleeves, emphasizing the emperor's affection and the intimate nature of their relationship.
  • 宫人皆效其断袖 - All the palace women imitated his practice of cutting off his sleeve, referring to the famous 'Dong Xian's Cut Sleeve' story, where the emperor cut off his sleeve to avoid waking Dong Xian.
  • 割袖 - Another way to refer to the 'cut sleeve' story, which is a metaphor for the emperor's deep affection for Dong Xian.
  • 恐惊其眠 - Fearing to disturb his sleep, explaining the reason behind the emperor's action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han, who ruled during the late Western Han dynasty and was known for his indulgence and favoritism towards Dong Xian.
  • 董贤 - A favorite official of Emperor Ai, known for his beauty and the emperor's deep affection for him.
  • 雾绡革衣 - Describes fine gauze and leather garments, which were considered luxurious and elegant.
  • 燕息之房 - Refers to a private room for rest and leisure.
  • 南门三重，署曰南中门、南上门、南更门 - Describes the three layers of the southern gate, each with its own name, indicating the grandeur and complexity of the palace.
  • 楼阁台榭，转相连注 - Describes the interconnected buildings and pavilions, emphasizing the architectural splendor of the palace.
  • 山池玩好，穷尽雕丽 - Refers to the gardens and ponds, which were meticulously designed and decorated.
  • 秦宫 - Refers to Qin Gong, a favorite of the powerful eunuch Liang Ji.
  • 梁冀 - A powerful eunuch and regent during the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his wealth and influence.
  • 孙寿 - The wife of Liang Ji, who also had a relationship with Qin Gong.
  • 李长吉 - A poet from the Tang dynasty, known for his imaginative and often surreal poetry.
  • 越罗衫袂迎春风 - Describes a light and airy garment made of silk, suitable for spring.
  • 玉刻麒麟腰带红 - Describes a red belt adorned with carved jade lions, symbolizing power and status.
  • 楼头曲宴仙人语 - Describes a banquet held on a high floor, where the conversations are as ethereal as those of immortals.
  • 帐底吹笙香雾浓 - Describes the luxurious and fragrant atmosphere of the tent, with music and incense creating a dreamlike setting.
  • 人间酒暖春茫茫 - Describes a warm and pleasant spring day, filled with the joy of drinking and socializing.
  • 花枝入帘白日长 - Describes the beauty of flowers entering the room, making the day seem longer and more enjoyable.
  • 飞窗复道传筹饮 - Describes the elaborate windows and corridors used for drinking games and socializing.
  • 午夜铜盘腻烛黄 - Describes the luxurious setting of a midnight feast, with golden candles and rich food.
  • 秃衿小袖调鹦鹉 - Describes the playful interaction with parrots, indicating a carefree and luxurious lifestyle.
  • 紫绣麻霞踏哮虎 - Describes the extravagant and dangerous activities, such as walking on tiger skins, symbolizing power and dominance.
  • 折桂销金待晓筵 - Describes the preparation for a morning banquet, with gold decorations and the scent of osmanthus.
  • 白鹿青苏半夜煮 - Describes the cooking of rare and exotic dishes, such as white deer and blue-green vegetables, indicating a luxurious and refined lifestyle.

  • 丁期婉娈 - Phrase meaning 'Ding Qi was graceful and charming.'

  • 坐食毕，便回盘与之 - Phrase meaning 'after finishing eating, he would return the plate to him.'

  • 玄临命之日，期乃以身捍刃 - Phrase meaning 'on the day Huan Xuan faced his death, Ding Qi protected him with his own body.'
  • 郑樱桃者，襄国优童也 - Phrase meaning 'Zheng Yaoyao was a young male entertainer from Xiangguo.'
  • 艳而善淫 - Phrase meaning 'he was handsome and skilled in seduction.'
  • 石虎为将军，绝嬖之 - Phrase meaning 'Shi Hu, as a general, was extremely fond of him.'
  • 以樱桃谮，杀其妻某氏 - Phrase meaning 'because of Zheng Yaoyao's slander, he killed his wife.'
  • 后娶某氏，复以樱桃谮杀之 - Phrase meaning 'after he remarried, he again killed his new wife because of Zheng Yaoyao's slander.'
  • 唐了颀有《郑樱桃歌》 - Phrase meaning 'Tang Lüqi wrote the *Song of Zheng Yaoyao*.'
  • 误以为妇人 - Phrase meaning 'mistakenly believing him to be a woman.'
  • 且不得其实 - Phrase meaning 'and not getting the real story.'
  • 第取其辞耳 - Phrase meaning 'only taking the words.'
  • 石季龙，僭天禄 - Phrase meaning 'Shi Jilong, usurping the heavenly mandate.'
  • 擅豪雄 - Phrase meaning 'arrogantly powerful.'
  • 美人姓郑名樱桃 - Phrase meaning 'the beautiful woman named Zheng Yaoyao.'
  • 樱桃美颜香且译 - Phrase meaning 'Zheng Yaoyao had a beautiful face and a sweet fragrance.'
  • 娥娥侍寝专宫掖 - Phrase meaning 'she served in the palace, always attending to the emperor's bedchamber.'
  • 后庭卷衣三万人 - Phrase meaning 'in the inner palace, there were three thousand people rolling up their clothes.'

[1025]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 少美姿容 - Young and handsome in appearance or young with beautiful features. A common way to describe youth and beauty in Classical Chinese.
  • 私款甚昵 - Had private affection and were very close. 私款 means private intimacy or private dealings, and 昵 means close or intimate.
  • 族子 - Cousin or nephew, depending on the exact familial relationship. Here, it likely means a cousin or younger relative within the same clan.
  • 避不往 - Avoided going or refused to go. Indicates that the person intentionally avoided the situation.
  • 潜于所住后作大坑 - Secretly dug a large pit behind his residence. 潜 means secretly or in secret, and 作大坑 is dug a large pit.
  • 欲诱确来则杀埋之 - Wanted to lure Wang Què to come and then kill and bury him. 诱 is to lure, and 杀埋之 is kill and bury him.
  • 从弟僧虔 - Cousin 僧虔 (Wang Shenqian). 从弟 is cousin or younger cousin (a male cousin).
  • 禁诃乃止 - Was forbidden and scolded, so it stopped. 禁诃 is forbidden and scolded, and 乃止 is then stopped or so it ceased.
  • 世微贱 - His family was humble and of low status. 世 can mean generation or family, and 微贱 means humble or lowly.
  • 业织履为生 - Made a living by weaving shoes. 业 means profession or trade, 织履 is weaving shoes, and 为生 is for a living.
  • 侯景乱 - The chaos of Hou Jing. Refers to the rebellion led by Hou Jing during the Liang dynasty.
  • 从父寓都下 - Followed his father to reside in the capital. 从父 can mean followed his father or a paternal uncle. 寓都下 means resided in the capital.
  • 尚总角 - Still a child or still in the total corner stage. 总角 is a term for childhood, when children tied their hair in two bunches.
  • 容貌艳丽，纤妍洁白如美妇人 - His appearance was beautiful and radiant, delicate and fair as a beautiful woman. 艳丽 is radiant or lustrous, 纤妍 is delicate and beautiful, and 洁白如美妇人 is white and fair like a beautiful woman.
  • 螓首膏发，自然蛾眉 - A forehead like a cicada, well-oiled hair, and naturally arched eyebrows. 螓首 is forehead like a cicada (a term for a beautiful face), 膏发 is well-oiled hair, and 蛾眉 is arched eyebrows or willow eyebrows.
  • 见者靡不啧啧 - All who saw him would comment in admiration. 见者 is those who saw him, 靡不 is none did not, and 啧啧 is to comment in amazement.
  • 即乱卒挥白刃，纵挥间噤不忍下 - Even the disorderly soldiers with their white blades, when they raised their weapons, hesitated and could not bring themselves to strike. 乱卒 is disorderly soldiers, 挥白刃 is swung their swords, and 噤不忍下 is could not bring themselves to strike.
  • 更引而出之数矣 - They repeatedly pulled him out of the situation. 更引而出之 is pulled him out again and again.
  • 陈司空霸先时平景乱 - When Chen Shikong (Chen Baiguan) pacified the Hou Jing rebellion. 陈司空 is the title of Chen Baiguan, 霸先 is his name, and 平景乱 is pacified the Hou Jing rebellion.
  • 其从子清以将军出镇吴兴 - His cousin Chen Qing was sent as a general to govern Wu Xing. 从子 is cousin, 以将军出镇 is was sent as a general to govern.
  • 子高于淮诸附部伍寄载求还乡 - Zhen Zigao sought to return to his hometown by attaching himself to a military unit and traveling by boat. 淮诸附部伍 is attached to the military units along the Huai River, and 寄载求还乡 is sought to return home by traveling on a boat.

  • 得无创巨汝耶 - A polite inquiry about potential harm, meaning 'Are you not afraid of hurting me greatly?'

  • 性恭谨，恒执佩身刀及待酒炙 - Describes behavior and duties, meaning 'He was naturally respectful and cautious, always holding his dagger and waiting to serve wine and roast meat.'
  • 性急，有所恚，目若虎，焰焰欲咬人 - Describes intense anger, meaning 'He was quick-tempered and when angry, his eyes looked like a tiger's, burning with the desire to bite.'
  • 见子高则立解 - Describes the calming effect of Zigao's presence, meaning 'Seeing Zigao, he immediately calmed down.'

  • 尝为诗赠之曰 - Introduces a poem, meaning 'He once wrote a poem to give him, saying.'
  • 昔闻周小史，今歌明下童 - Refers to historical and contemporary figures, meaning 'In the past, I heard of the Zhou historian; now I sing of the bright young boy.'

  • 人言吾有帝王相，审尔，当册汝为后，但恐同姓致嫌耳 - A conditional statement with a hint of concern, meaning 'People say I have the appearance of a king or emperor. If that is true, I will make you my queen, but I fear that being of the same surname might cause jealousy.'
  • 古有女主，当亦有男后 - A historical reference to gender roles, meaning 'In ancient times there were female rulers, so there should also be male consorts.'
  • 明公果垂异恩，奴亦何辞作吴孟子耶 - A humble and respectful response, meaning 'If you truly bestow this special favor, I will not refuse to be your Fuzi (a term of respect).'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 願得炎州無霜色，出入歡袖百千年 - Poetic expression wishing for a place to be free of frost and for joy to last for centuries. '出入歡袖' is metaphorical, suggesting joy should be present both inside and outside, or in all aspects of life.
  • 事漸泄，所不知者司空而已 - Statement indicating the matter was gradually revealed, and only the official (司空) was unaware. '所不知者' is a nominalized clause meaning 'the one who does not know.'
  • 會王僧辯有母喪，未及為禮娶 - Statement about Wang Sengbian having a mother's death and not yet having time to perform the proper mourning rites before marriage. '未及為禮' means 'not yet had time to perform the proper rites.'
  • 忿恨以语僧辩，用他事停司空女婚 - Statement about Zihao expressing anger to Wang Sengbian and using another matter to stop the marriage of the official's daughter. '用他事' means 'using another matter.'
  • 司空怒，且谓僧辩之见图也 - Statement about the official being angry and believing that Wang Sengbian was plotting against him. '见图' means 'to plot against.'
  • 遂发兵袭僧辩并其子，缢杀之 - Statement about the official sending troops to attack Wang Sengbian and his son, killing them by strangulation. '缢杀' means 'killed by strangulation.'
  • 率子高实为军锋焉 - Statement about Zihao leading the troops as the vanguard. '军锋' means 'vanguard of the army.'
  • 自是子高引避不敢人 - Statement about Zihao then avoiding and not daring to approach. '引避' means 'to avoid.'
  • 知之，仍领子高之镇 - Statement about someone knowing about it and still leading Zihao to the town. '领子高之镇' means 'leading Zihao to the town.'
  • 女以念极，结气死 - Statement about the official's daughter dying from excessive grief. '结气死' means 'died from grief.'
  • 司空为武帝崩，后从犹子入嗣大统 - Statement about the official becoming the successor to the throne after the death of Emperor Wu, following his nephew. '嗣大统' means 'successor to the throne.'
  • 子高为右卫将军散骑常侍，积功封文招县子 - Statement about Zihao becoming a Right Guard General and a Scattered Rider Attendant, accumulating merit and being granted the title of Marquis of Wenzhao. '积功封' means 'accumulating merit and being granted a title.'
  • 废帝时，坐诬谋反诛 - Statement about Zihao being executed during the reign of the deposed emperor for falsely plotting a rebellion. '坐诬谋反' means 'being executed for falsely plotting a rebellion.'
  • 人以为隐报焉 - Statement about people believing it was a hidden retribution. '隐报' means 'hidden retribution.'
  • 王韶字德茂，少美丽，善姿首 - Statement about Wang Shao, whose name is Dezhao, and who was handsome and well-formed. '善姿首' means 'well-formed appearance.'
  • 初袭父封都乡侯，为太子舍人，累迁郢州刺史 - Statement about Wang Shao initially inheriting his father's title of Duke of Duxiang, serving as a Gentleman of the Crown Prince's Household, and later being promoted to Governor of Yingshou. '袭父封' means 'inheriting his father's title.'
  • 韶其为幼童，庾开府信爱之，有断袖之欢，衣食所资，皆信所给 - Statement about Wang Shao, when he was a young child, being loved by Yu Kai-fu and having a close relationship, with Yu providing for his clothing and food. '断袖之欢' means 'a close relationship.'
  • 遇客，韶亦为信侍酒 - Statement about when guests were present, Wang Shao also served as a companion to Yu. '侍酒' means 'to serve as a companion.'
  • 后为郢州 - Statement about Wang Shao later becoming the Governor of Yingshou.
  • 信西上江陵，途经江夏，韶接信甚薄，坐青油幕下，引信入宴，坐信别榻，有自矜色 - Statement about Yu traveling west to Jiangling, passing through Jiangxia, where Wang Shao received him poorly, seating him separately under a blue oil canopy, and showing a self-important demeanor. '青油幕' means 'blue oil canopy.'
  • 宾客满座，韶甚惭耻 - Statement about guests filling the room, and Wang Shao feeling very ashamed. '宾客满座' means 'guests filling the room.'

  • 庾开府 - Historical figure, Yu Kai-fu, a high-ranking official during the Southern Dynasties period.

  • 绝大愤痛，誓不徒还 - He was extremely angry and sad, vowing not to return without his wife.

  • 日往四通，即深凌险以索之 - Every day, he went in all directions, delving deep into the dangerous terrain to search for her.
  • 既逾月，忽于百里之外丛问，得其妻绣履一只 - After more than a month, he suddenly found one of his wife's embroidered shoes in a dense forest a hundred miles away.
  • 虽浸雨濡，犹可辨识 - Although it was soaked with rain, it could still be recognized.
  • 纥尤凄悼，求之益坚 - Ouyang He was especially sad and determined to find her even more.
  • 选壮士三十人，持兵负粮，岩栖野食 - He selected thirty strong men, armed with weapons and carrying food, to live in caves and eat in the wild.
  • 又旬余，远所舍约二百里，南望一山，葱秀迥出 - After another ten days, they were about two hundred miles away from their camp. Looking south, they saw a mountain standing out prominently.
  • 至其下，有深溪环之 - When they reached the base of the mountain, there was a deep ravine surrounding it.
  • 乃编木以渡 - They tied wooden planks together to cross the ravine.
  • 绝岩翠竹之间，时见红彩，闻笑语声 - Between the sheer cliffs and green bamboo, they occasionally saw red colors and heard laughter and voices.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 梁大同未 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang (梁武帝) during the early Liang Dynasty (502–557 CE). '大同' is the era name of Emperor Wu, and '未' indicates the time before the end of this era.
  • 平南将军蔺钦 - 'General of the South, Lin Qin,' was a military officer during the Liang Dynasty. '平南将军' is a title for a general in charge of southern campaigns.
  • 李师古、陈彻 - These are the names of two leaders who were defeated by Lin Qin. They were likely local warlords or rebels during the Liang Dynasty.
  • 欧阳纥 - 'Ouyang He' was a general during the Liang Dynasty. He is the main character in this story.
  • 长乐 - A place name, likely a region or city that was conquered by Ouyang He.
  • 绣履 - 'Embroidered shoes' are a symbol of luxury and status in Chinese culture.

  • 待吾计成，招之即出 - 'Wait until my plan is complete, then call it out.'
  • 屏气以俟 - 'Hold one’s breath in waiting.'
  • 日晡 - Classical term for the time of day, roughly 'afternoon' or 'the time of the western sun.'
  • 有物如匹练 - 'There was something like a ribbon of silk,' referring to a long, flowing object.
  • 自他山下，透至若飞 - 'Coming from another mountain, passing through as if flying.'
  • 径入洞中 - 'Directly entering the cave.'
  • 少选 - Classical term meaning 'a short while later' or 'after a while.'
  • 美髯丈夫 - 'A bearded man of fine appearance.'
  • 长六尺余 - 'Six chi plus in height,' with 'chi' being an ancient Chinese unit of length (approximately 0.33 meters).
  • 白衣曳杖 - 'Wearing white clothes and dragging a staff.'
  • 拥诸妇人而出 - 'Carrying out a group of women.'
  • 见犬惊视 - 'Seeing the dog, it looked in surprise.'
  • 腾身执之 - 'Leapt up and seized it.'
  • 披裂吮咀 - 'Tore it open and sucked it.'
  • 食之致饱 - 'Ate it until full.'
  • 妇人竟以玉杯进酒 - 'The women finally offered wine in jade cups.'
  • 谐笑甚欢 - 'Laughed and joked very happily.'
  • 既饮数斗 - 'After drinking several dou (a unit of volume).'
  • 扶之而去 - 'Carried it away.'
  • 又闻嬉笑之音 - 'Again heard the sound of laughter.'
  • 良久 - 'After a long time.'
  • 乃持刃而入 - 'Then took a blade and entered.'
  • 见大白猿 - 'Saw a large white ape.'
  • 缚四足于床头 - 'Bound its four feet to the head of the bed.'
  • 顾人蹙缩求脱 - 'Looking at the person, it crouched and tried to escape.'
  • 目光如电 - 'Its eyes were like lightning.'
  • 竟兵之，如中铁石 - 'Finally struck it with the weapon, as if it were iron and stone.'
  • 刺其脐下，即饮刃 - 'Stabbed below its navel, and it immediately drank the blade.'
  • 血射如注 - 'Blood sprayed like a stream.'
  • 乃大叹咤曰 - 'Then he greatly exclaimed and said.'
  • 此天杀我，岂尔之能 - 'This is heaven killing me, not your ability.'
  • 然尔妇已孕，勿杀其子 - 'But your wife is pregnant, do not kill her child.'
  • 将逢圣帝，必大其宗 - 'He will meet a sage emperor, and his clan will be greatly expanded.'
  • 言绝乃死 - 'After his words ended, he died.'
  • 搜其藏 - 'Searched its treasure.'
  • 宝器丰积 - 'Precious objects were abundantly stored.'
  • 珍羞盈品 - 'Delicacies were full in variety.'
  • 罗列几案 - 'Arranged on tables and desks.'
  • 凡人世所珍，靡不充备 - 'All things precious in the world were fully provided.'
  • 名香数斛 - 'Several hu of famous fragrant incense' (a hu is an ancient unit of volume).
  • 宝剑一双 - 'A pair of precious swords.'
  • 妇人三十辈，皆绝色 - 'Thirty women, all of extraordinary beauty.'
  • 久者至十年 - 'The longest [one] had been there for ten years.'
  • 云色衰必被提去 - 'It is said that when their beauty fades, they are taken away.'
  • 莫知所置 - 'No one knows where they are placed.'
  • 又捕采惟止其身 - 'Moreover, the capture is only of the body.'
  • 更亡党类 - 'They do not have companions or kin.'
  • 但盥洗，着帽，加白袷，被素罗衣 - 'They only wash, put on a hat, wear a white jacket, and are wrapped in a white silk robe.'
  • 不知寒暑 - 'They do not know cold or heat.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 食凛 - Likely a rare or archaic term, possibly a specific place or resource associated with the creature. May be a misspelling or variant of '食廪' (food store) or '食林' (food forest), but uncertain.
  • 白猿 - A white ape, often associated with mythological or supernatural beings in Chinese folklore. In some stories, white apes are linked to immortality or magical powers.
  • 圣帝 - 'Sage emperor,' a title used for a ruler of moral and political perfection, often in mythical or prophetic contexts.
  • 玉杯 - Jade cups, which were highly valued in ancient China for both their material and symbolic value.
  • 白袷 - A white jacket or robe, possibly a type of traditional clothing.
  • 素罗衣 - A robe made of plain silk or fine fabric, likely indicating a refined or otherworldly appearance.
  • 名香数斛 - 'Famous fragrant incense in several hu' – incense was often used in rituals and for spiritual or medicinal purposes.
  • 妇人三十辈 - 'Thirty women' – the term '辈' here may mean 'generation' or 'set,' but in this context, it likely refers to a group of thirty women.
  • 色衰必被提去 - The idea that when a woman’s beauty fades, she is taken away by supernatural forces. This echoes themes in Chinese mythology where immortal beings or supernatural beings may take human women, especially beautiful ones, into their realm.
  • 不知寒暑 - This may suggest that these women are not bound by the physical constraints of the natural world, possibly indicating supernatural or immortal status.

[1032]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遍身白毛，長數寸 - Describes a physical characteristic of having white fur all over the body, several inches long.
  • 所居常讀木簡，字若符篆，了不可識 - Refers to the creature's habit of reading wooden tablets with characters resembling ancient scripts, which are completely unreadable.
  • 已則置石磴下 - Indicates that after reading, the creature places the tablets under a stone step.
  • 晴昼或舞雙劍，環身電飛，光圓若月 - Describes the creature's behavior of dancing with two swords during sunny days, creating a circular light around its body like the moon.
  • 其飲食無常，喜啖果栗，尤嗜犬，咀而飲其血 - States that the creature's diet is unpredictable, but it enjoys eating fruits, chestnuts, and especially dogs, chewing and drinking their blood.
  • 日始逾午，即然而逝 - Means that after noon, the creature disappears.
  • 半昼往返數千里，及晚必歸 - Describes the creature's ability to travel thousands of miles in half a day and return by evening.
  • 夜就諸床嬲戲，一夕皆周，未嘗寢寐 - Indicates that at night, the creature plays with various beds, visiting each one in a single night without ever sleeping.
  • 言語淹詳，華旨會和 - Describes the creature's speech as eloquent and harmonious.
  • 然其狀，即暇狸之類也 - Concludes that despite these abilities, the creature resembles a kind of cat.
  • 吾為山神所訴，將得死罪 - The creature states that it has been accused by a mountain deity and faces death.
  • 亦求護之於眾靈，庶幾可免 - The creature seeks protection from various spirits, hoping to avoid punishment.
  • 前月哉生魄，石燈生火，焚其簡書 - Describes an event where a stone lamp ignites, burning the creature's tablets.
  • 吾已千歲，而無子。今有子，死期至矣 - The creature laments that after a thousand years without offspring, it now has a child, but its own death is imminent.
  • 因顧諸女，仇瀾者久之 - The creature looks at its daughters, lingering on one named Chou Lan.
  • 此山復絕，未嘗有人至。上高而望，絕不見樵者，下多虎狼怪獸 - Describes the isolation of the mountain, stating that it is so remote that no one ever visits, and from the top, one cannot see a single woodcutter, while the lower areas are inhabited by tigers, wolves, and other strange beasts.
  • 今能至者，非天假之何耶 - The creature wonders how anyone could reach such a place unless it were by divine intervention.
  • 及長，果文學善書，知名於時 - States that the creature's son grew up to be a scholar and calligrapher, becoming well-known in his time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山神 - Refers to a mountain deity, a common figure in Chinese folklore and mythology.
  • 眾靈 - Refers to various spirits or deities, often invoked in Chinese religious and folk practices.
  • 石燈生火 - Refers to a stone lamp igniting, which could be a supernatural event or a metaphor for divine intervention.
  • 暇狸 - A type of cat, possibly a mythical or legendary creature.
  • 江總 - A historical figure, likely a literary or political figure of the time.
  • 陳武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Chen, a ruler of the Southern Dynasties period.

Proper Nouns

  • 山神 - Mountain deity.
  • 眾靈 - Various spirits or deities.
  • 石燈 - Stone lamp.
  • 暇狸 - A type of cat.
  • 江總 - Historical figure.
  • 陳武帝 - Emperor Wu of Chen.

  • 文华极盛之弊 - Refers to the drawbacks of extreme literary prosperity; used to critique the tendency of Tang writers to create strange stories.

  • 谐噱 - Means 'humorous laughter'; used to describe the value of stories in providing humor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长孙太尉 - Refers to Zhangsun Wuji, a high-ranking official and military leader during the Tang dynasty.
  • 麟阁 - The Hall of Merit, a place where portraits of meritorious officials were hung.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 彼見是忘憂，此看同腐草 - Metaphorical expression comparing the perceived differences between two things. '彼' (that) and '此' (this) contrast two perspectives, while '忘憂' (forgetting worries) and '腐草' (rotten grass) represent the extremes of value or worth.
  • 青山與白雲 - Poetic image of natural beauty, often used to evoke a sense of peace and serenity.

  • 吟讽既毕 - Means 'after reciting and commenting,' indicating the completion of a literary or poetic activity.
  • 容色惨然 - Describes a somber or sad expression on someone's face.
  • 褰帘 - Means 'to lift the curtain,' often used to indicate a change in scene or a revelation.
  • 税居之士 - Refers to a person who has rented a house, often used to describe someone of modest means.
  • 故袁长官之女 - Refers to the daughter of a former official named Yuan, indicating her noble background.
  • 少孤 - Means 'lost her parents at a young age,' describing a person who grew up without parental care.
  • 无姻戚 - Means 'no relatives or in-laws,' indicating a lack of family support.
  • 小娘子 - A respectful term for a young woman, often used by older women or servants.

  • 美艳愈于向者所睹 - Means 'more beautiful than what was seen before,' emphasizing the enhanced beauty of the woman.
  • 命侍婢进茶果 - Means 'ordered the maidservant to bring tea and fruit,' indicating a polite gesture.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 青山与白云 - Common motif in Chinese literature, often used to symbolize purity, tranquility, and the natural world.
  • 吟讽 - Refers to the practice of reciting and commenting on poetry or literature, a common activity among literati in ancient China.
  • 税居之士 - Indicates a person of modest means, often used to describe someone who has rented a house rather than owning one.
  • 少孤 - Describes a person who grew up without parental care, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 无姻戚 - Indicates a lack of family support, which was a significant social and economic issue in ancient China.
  • 小娘子 - A respectful term for a young woman, often used by older women or servants, reflecting the hierarchical nature of Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Proper Nouns

[1036]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 识用渐隳 - Knowledge and ability gradually deteriorate.
  • 律液倾输 - The essence of the law flows out, suggesting a loss of integrity or principle.
  • 根蒂浮动 - The roots and foundation are unstable, indicating a lack of solid grounding.
  • 骨将化上 - The bones will dissolve upward, a metaphor for the body's disintegration or the weakening of one's core.
  • 颜非渥丹人必为怪异所铄 - If the face is not rosy, one will surely be scorched by strange phenomena, suggesting that without a healthy appearance, one will be affected by supernatural or unusual events.
  • 何坚隐而不剖其由也 - Why is it so stubbornly hidden without revealing its cause? This is a rhetorical question expressing frustration or confusion.
  • 弟之忖度，何以为异 - What is so different about my estimation? This is a rhetorical question expressing surprise or disagreement.
  • 岂有袁氏海内无瓜葛之亲哉 - How could there be no relatives of the Yuan family in the entire world? This is a rhetorical question expressing disbelief.
  • 辩慧多能，如是以为验 - With much intelligence and ability, it is considered a test, suggesting that the person's skills are seen as a challenge or a way to prove something.
  • 久处冻馁 - Long suffering from cold and hunger, indicating prolonged hardship.
  • 颇似苏息 - Somewhat like rest or relief, suggesting a temporary improvement in one's condition.
  • 不能负义，何以为什 - How can one not betray righteousness, and what is the point of it? This is a rhetorical question expressing moral confusion.
  • 大丈夫未能事人，焉能事鬼 - A true man cannot serve others, how can he serve ghosts? This is a rhetorical question expressing a rejection of supernatural or mystical practices.
  • 妖由人兴，人无妖焉 - Evil spirits arise from people, but people do not have evil spirits, suggesting that supernatural phenomena are caused by human actions.
  • 义与身孰亲 - Which is closer, righteousness or one's body? This is a rhetorical question expressing a moral dilemma.
  • 身受其灾，而顾其鬼怪之恩义 - One suffers from disasters but thinks of the favor and righteousness of ghosts, suggesting a misplaced focus on supernatural beings rather than one's own well-being.
  • 三尺童子，尚以为不可 - Even a three-foot child would consider it wrong, expressing that even a young child would find the action morally unacceptable.
  • 宝剑，亦干将之俦亚也 - The treasure sword, also a peer of Gan Jiang, referring to a famous swordsmith and suggesting that the sword is of high quality.
  • 魍魉，见者灭没 - Wangliang, who disappears when seen, referring to a type of ghost or spirit that vanishes when observed.
  • 诘朝奉借 - Borrow it tomorrow, suggesting a request for the sword to be lent the next day.
  • 倘携密适，必睹其狼狈 - If taken to a secret place, one will surely see its disarray, suggesting that the sword's presence will cause chaos or confusion.
  • 昔日回君携宝镜而照鹦鹉也 - As when the lord took the precious mirror to reflect the parrot, referring to a past event where a mirror was used to reflect an image, suggesting a comparison to the current situation.

  • 别来数十年，僧行夏腊极高，能别形骸，善出尘垢 - Means 'It has been several decades since we last met. Monk Hui has reached a very high level of seniority and is able to distinguish forms and bodies, and is skilled at removing worldly impurities.'
  • 倘经彼设食，颇益南行之福 - Means 'If we could have a meal prepared by him, it would greatly benefit our journey south.'

  • 及抵寺，袁氏欣然易服、理鬓，携二子诣老僧院，若熟其径者 - Means 'When they arrived at the temple, Mrs. Yuan happily changed her clothes, tidied her hair, and took her two sons to the old monk's hall, as if she were familiar with the path.'
  • 恪颇异之 - Means 'Ke found this very strange.'

  • 及斋罢，有野猿数十，连臂下于高松而食于台上，后悲啸扪萝而跃 - Means 'After the meal, there were dozens of wild monkeys who linked arms and descended from the tall pines to eat on the terrace. Later, they mournfully called and leaped up the vines.'

  • 俄命笔题僧壁曰 - Means 'Soon, she asked to write on the wall of the monk's room.'

  • 云：“郎君颇闻异香，某辈所闻，但蛇臊不可近” - They said, 'Sir, you smelled a strange fragrance, but we smelled only the stench of snakes, which was unbearable,' where '颇闻' means to smell, and '蛇臊' means the stench of snakes.

  • 但见小蛇数条，尽白色，皆杀之而归 - They only saw several small white snakes, which they all killed and brought back, where '尽白色' means all white, and '皆杀之' means all killed them.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 君将适江南 - Classical expression meaning 'You are going to the south of the river (Jiangnan).'
  • 路出其墓下 - 'The path will pass beneath her tomb.' '出' here means 'to pass through' or 'to go through.'
  • 以妾之故，必为其所困 - 'Because of me, you will surely be troubled by her.' '妾' is a humble term for 'I' used by a woman; '必为其所困' is a passive construction meaning 'will surely be troubled by her.'
  • 书符 - Refers to the practice of writing talismans.
  • 制鬼神 - A common Daoist term for controlling supernatural beings.
  • 君若求之即免矣 - 'If you seek him out, you will be free from this.' '免矣' is a classical way of saying 'will be free' or 'will be saved.'
  • 妾居此亦终不安 - 'I cannot remain here either; I will never be at peace.' '终不安' is a concise way of saying 'will never be at peace.'
  • 挈我俱去 - 'Take me with you.' '挈' is a classical term for 'to take along' or 'to bring with.'
  • 置我洛阳北坂上 - 'Place me on the northern slope of Luoyang.' '坂' refers to a hill or slope.
  • 得与君相近，永有依托，生成之惠也 - 'To be near you, to have eternal support—this would be a great favor.' '生成之惠' is a formal expression for 'a great favor' or 'a blessing.'
  • 迁葬之札 - 'The matter of relocating the burial, is a matter of the Mu family.' '札' can mean 'document' or 'matter'; in this context, it refers to the formal matter of relocation.
  • 酒酣 - 'When the wine was flowing freely' or 'when drunk.'
  • 露草芊芊 - 'The dewy grasses are green.' '芊芊' is a poetic term for lush or green grass.
  • 自我居此，于今几年 - A rhetorical question expressing the passage of time.
  • 与君先祖，畴昔恩波，死生契阔，忽此相过 - 'With your ancestors, there was once a bond of deep affection; through life and death, we were connected—now suddenly this meeting.' '畴昔' means 'in the past,' '恩波' is a poetic term for 'favor' or 'grace,' '契阔' is a classical term for 'close relationship' or 'fidelity.'
  • 谁谓佳期，寻当别离 - 'Who would have thought this would be a time of joy, only to be followed by separation?' '佳期' is a poetic term for 'happy time' or 'auspicious meeting.'
  • 俟君之北，携手同归 - 'When you go north, we will walk together and return.' '俟' means 'await' or 'when.'
  • 因下泪沾襟 - 'She wept, tears soaking her robe.' '沾襟' is a classical way of saying 'tears soaked her robe.'
  • 独孤郎勿负县主厚意 - 'Sir Dugulu, do not betray the county princess’s kindness.' '厚意' is a term for 'kindness' or 'favor.'
  • 清江桂舟 - 'The clear river with its fragrant boats, where we may roam freely.' '桂舟' is a poetic term for a boat made of fragrant wood, or a boat in a scenic river.
  • 不遑淹留 - 'No time to linger.' '不遑' means 'no time for,' and '淹留' means 'to linger.'
  • 一辱佳贶，永以为好 - 'To receive your generous gift is a great honor; I will cherish it forever.' '辱' is a humble term for 'to receive,' '佳贶' is 'generous gift,' '永以为好' is 'to cherish forever as a friend.'
  • 须臾，天将明 - 'Soon, the sky was about to dawn.' '须臾' is a classical term for 'in a moment' or 'soon.'
  • 凡在坐者，皆与辞诀 - 'All those present bid farewell.' '辞诀' is a formal term for 'farewell' or 'take leave.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江南 - Refers to the southern region of the Yangtze River, a historically significant and culturally rich area in ancient China.
  • 道士王善交 - A Daoist practitioner named Wang Shanjiao, who practiced talisman-writing (书符) in the market of 淮南 (Huainan), a region in present-day Anhui province.
  • 县主 - A noble title for a princess or high-ranking woman in a county, often a daughter of a high official or noble family.
  • 独孤郎 - A name or honorific used for a man, possibly a reference to the Dugu clan, a prominent family in northern China during the Tang dynasty.
  • 洛阳北坂 - The northern slope of Luoyang, a major city in ancient China and the capital of several dynasties, including the Han and Tang.
  • 迁葬之札 - A formal document or matter concerning the relocation of a burial, which was a significant and ritualized act in ancient Chinese society.
  • 酒酣 - A classical expression for being drunk or in a state of deep intoxication, often used in literature to describe emotional or poetic moments.
  • 露草芊芊 - A poetic image of dew-covered grass, often used in classical poetry to evoke a melancholic or romantic atmosphere.
  • 颓茔未迁 - A description of an old, ruined tomb that has not yet been moved, suggesting the passage of time and the need for relocation.
  • 死生契阔 - A classical phrase from the *Book of Songs* (*Shijing*), meaning 'through life and death, we remain faithful' or 'bound by life and death.'
  • 清江桂舟 - A poetic image of a boat on a clear river, often associated with leisure, travel, or romantic imagery.
  • 不遑淹留 - A classical expression meaning 'no time to linger,' often used in poetry or prose to express urgency or the need to move on.
  • 一辱佳贶 - A humble expression meaning 'to receive your generous gift,' with '辱' being a humble term used to express gratitude or modesty.

[1155]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既出门，回顾无所见 - Literal description of a sudden change in perception; 'Having gone out, he looked back and saw nothing.'
  • 地平坦，亦无坟墓之迹 - Emphasizes the unexpectedness of the scene; 'The ground was flat, with no trace of a grave.'
  • 穆意恍惚，良久乃定 - Describes a state of shock or confusion; 'Mu's mind was dazed, and it took a long time for him to regain composure.'
  • 因徙柳树一株以志之 - Symbolic act to remember the location; 'He then moved a willow tree to mark the spot.'
  • 家人索穆颇急 - Highlights the concern of his family; 'His family was searching for him with great urgency.'
  • 后数日，穆乃入淮南市 - Indicates a change in location; 'After several days, Mu entered the market in Huainan.'
  • 果遇王善交于市 - Fortuitous encounter; 'He indeed met Wang Shanjiao in the market.'
  • 遂求一符 - Seeking a protective or magical object; 'He then asked for a talisman.'
  • 既至恶王墓下，为旋风所扑三四 - Describes a supernatural event; 'After arriving at the evil Wang's tomb, he was struck by whirlwinds three or four times.'
  • 穆因出符示之乃止 - Indicates the talisman's effectiveness; 'Mu then showed the talisman to it, and it ceased.'
  • 先是，穆颇不信鬼神之事 - Sets up a contrast with his later experiences; 'Previously, Mu did not believe in ghosts and spirits.'
  • 及县主无不明晓，穆乃深叹讶 - Shows his reaction to the revelation; 'When the county magistrate became clear, Mu deeply sighed and was astonished.'
  • 亦私为所亲者言之 - Personal sharing of the experience; 'He also privately told those close to him.'
  • 次年正月，自江南回，发其地数尺，得骸骨一具 - Describes a physical discovery; 'The following January, upon returning from Jiangnan, he dug up the site for several feet and found a set of bones.'
  • 以衣衾敛之 - Respectful act of burial; 'He wrapped them in clothing and a shroud.'
  • 穆以其死时草草，葬必有阙 - Shows his concern for the deceased; 'Mu thought that since he died hastily, his burial must have been incomplete.'
  • 既至洛阳，大具威仪，亲为祝文以祭之 - Formal and respectful burial; 'Upon arriving in Luoyang, he prepared a grand ceremony, personally wrote a prayer text to offer sacrifices.'
  • 葬于安喜门外 - Specifies the burial location; 'He buried him outside the Anxi Gate.'
  • 其后独宿于村野，县主复至 - Recurring supernatural presence; 'Later, when he was alone in the countryside, the county magistrate came again.'
  • 谓穆曰：“迁葬之德，万古不忘，幽滞之人，分不及此者久矣。” - Statement of gratitude and acknowledgment; 'He said to Mu, 'The virtue of relocating the grave will be remembered for a thousand years. For a person of the underworld, it has been long since they could not attain this.''
  • 幸君惠存旧好，使我永得安宅 - Expression of gratitude; 'Thank you for your kindness in preserving our old friendship, allowing me to always have a peaceful home.'
  • 道途之间，所不奉见者，以君为我腐秽，恐致嫌恶耳 - Explains the reason for his absence; 'In the world, I was not seen, fearing that you would regard me as corrupt and filthy, which would lead to aversion.'
  • 穆睹其车舆导从，悉光赫于当时 - Describes the grandeur of the county magistrate's appearance; 'Mu saw his carriage and attendants, all of whom were splendid at the time.'
  • 县主谢曰：“此皆君子赐也。岁至己卯，当遂相见。” - Promise of future reunion; 'The county magistrate thanked him, saying, 'All of this is a gift from a gentleman. When the year of Jǐ Mǎo arrives, we will surely meet again.''
  • 其夕因宿穆所，至明乃去 - Brief visit; 'That night, he stayed at Mu's place and left at dawn.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 县主 - Refers to a county magistrate, a local government official in ancient China.
  • 淮南市 - Refers to a market in Huainan, a region in modern-day Anhui Province.
  • 王善交 - A person named Wang Shanjiao, likely a local figure or acquaintance.
  • 恶王墓 - Refers to the tomb of an evil Wang, possibly a local legend or historical figure.
  • 安喜门 - Refers to the Anxi Gate, a specific gate in Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 己卯 - Refers to the year of Jǐ Mǎo, a specific year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 炜出艾试灸之，应手而落 - Literal description of a medical treatment using moxibustion with mugwort (艾), where the ailment is cured instantly upon application.
  • 贫道无以奉酬 - A humble expression meaning 'I have nothing to offer in return,' often used by monks or Taoists.
  • 藏镪巨万 - Euphemistic expression meaning 'hoarding vast wealth,' where '镪' is an old term for money.
  • 君子能疗之，当有厚报 - A polite way of saying 'If you can cure him, you will receive a generous reward.'
  • 素善丝竹 - Classical way of saying 'has always been skilled in music,' where '丝竹' refers to string and wind instruments.
  • 闻主人堂中琴声 - Literally 'hearing the sound of the zither in the master's hall,' indicating the presence of music in the household.
  • 门下客既无血属 - Euphemistic way of saying 'the guest is not related by blood,' implying the guest is expendable.
  • 可以为飨 - Ritualistic expression meaning 'can be offered as a sacrifice or feast.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 艾试灸之 - Refers to the traditional Chinese medical practice of moxibustion, where dried mugwort (艾) is burned to stimulate healing.
  • 贫道 - A term used by Taoist monks or practitioners, meaning 'this humble monk' or 'this humble Taoist.'
  • 转经 - A Buddhist practice involving the recitation or chanting of sutras to accumulate merit.
  • 藏镪巨万 - Reflects the cultural emphasis on wealth and its accumulation in ancient Chinese society.
  • 门下客 - Refers to a guest or subordinate in a household, often in a feudal or aristocratic context.
  • 毒神 - A ritualistic practice where sacrifices are made to appease or honor deities.
  • 飨之 - A ritualistic offering, often involving food or other goods, to deities or ancestors.

Proper Nouns

  • 炜 - The name of the protagonist, likely a healer or practitioner.
  • 任翁 - The name of the wealthy man suffering from an ailment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尝闻大恩尚不报，况愈小疾乎！ - Rhetorical question expressing the idea that even great kindness is not always repaid, let alone lesser favors.
  • 已潜扃炜所处之室，而炜不之悟。 - Secretly lock the room where Wei is staying, Wei does not realize.
  • 是女密知之，潜持刀于窗隙间告炜曰： - The girl secretly knows, holds a knife between window gaps, and tells Wei.
  • 吾家事鬼，今夜当杀汝而祭之。 - Our family deals with spirits, tonight we will kill you and sacrifice you.
  • 汝可以此破窗遁去，不然少顷死矣！ - You can use this to break the window and escape, otherwise you will die shortly.
  • 此刀亦望将去，无相累也。 - Take this knife with you, so it won't burden you.
  • 炜闻恐悸流汗，以刀断窗棂，携艾跃出，拔键而走。 - Wei hears and becomes fearful and anxious, breaks the window with a knife, carries mugwort and jumps out, pulls the lock and runs away.
  • 任翁俄觉，率家童十余人，持刀秉炬，逐之六七里，几及之。 - Mr. Ren suddenly realizes, leads more than ten servants, holds knives and torches, chases Wei for six to seven li, almost catches him.
  • 炜因迷道失足，坠于大枯井中。 - Wei loses his way and stumbles, falls into a large dry well.
  • 追者失踪而返。 - The pursuers lose track of Wei and return.
  • 伟虽坠井，为槁叶所藉幸而不伤。 - Wei falls into the well, is supported by dried leaves, and is luckily not injured.
  • 及晓视之，乃一巨穴，深百余丈，无计得出。 - By morning, it is discovered to be a large cave, more than a hundred zhang deep, with no way to get out.

  • 任翁 - Refers to Mr. Ren, likely a character in the story or a historical figure.

[1159]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 光照穴中 - Literal translation: 'Light shines into the cave.' '光照' can imply a supernatural or divine light, suggesting a mystical or miraculous setting.
  • 前有石臼 - 'There is a stone mortar in front.' '石臼' is a stone mortar or basin, often used in ancient Chinese for grinding grain or holding liquids.
  • 岩上有物滴下，如饴蜜 - 'Something drips from the rock above, like honey or syrup.' This is a metaphorical description of a viscous, sweet liquid, possibly symbolic or magical.
  • 注召集臼中 - 'It flows and collects into the mortar.' The verb '注集' combines 'to pour' and 'to gather,' indicating a continuous flow into the mortar.
  • 蛇就饮之 - 'The snake goes to drink it.' This is a simple but culturally significant phrase, as snakes are often symbolic in Chinese literature.
  • 炜察蛇有异 - 'Wěi observed that the snake was unusual.' '察' means to observe or notice, and '有异' means 'was strange' or 'was different.'
  • 乃诣蛇稽颡谓之曰 - 'He then went to the snake and bowed his forehead to it, saying...' This is a formal and reverent gesture, indicating deep respect or supplication.
  • “龙王，某不幸，堕于此，愿王悯之，而不为害！” - 'Lord Dragon King, I am unfortunate and have fallen here. I beg you, Lord, to have mercy on me and not harm me!' This is a formal supplication to a deity or supernatural being.
  • 因饮其余，遂不饥渴 - 'He then drank the remainder, and thus no longer felt hunger or thirst.' This implies that the liquid has a magical or divine property.
  • 细视蛇之唇吻，亦有疣焉 - 'Carefully observing the snake’s lips, he saw a warty growth.' '疣' refers to a warty or tumor-like growth, possibly symbolic of a curse or illness.
  • 炜感蛇见悯，欲为灸之 - 'Wěi was moved by the snake’s compassion and wanted to treat it with moxibustion.' This shows empathy and a desire to help the supernatural being.
  • 而无烛不遂 - 'But without a candle, he could not proceed.' This is a practical problem, but in a magical context, it may imply a lack of spiritual or divine light.
  • 须臾，忽有飘火入穴 - 'In a moment, suddenly a floating fire entered the cave.' This is a magical or divine intervention.
  • 伟乃燃艾启蛇而灸 - 'Wěi then lit the mugwort and began moxibustion on the snake.' This is a medical procedure, but in this context, it is performed on a supernatural being.
  • 则疣应手坠地 - 'And the warty growth fell to the ground at his touch.' This implies a successful and immediate magical effect.
  • 蛇之饮食久已妨碍，及去，颇以为适 - 'The snake had long been hindered by its diet, and when it was removed, it felt quite comfortable.' This suggests that the warty growth was causing the snake discomfort.
  • 遂吐径寸珠酬炜 - 'Then it spat out a pearl a inch in diameter to repay Wěi.' This is a supernatural gift, symbolizing gratitude and recognition of Wěi’s kindness.
  • 炜不受，而启蛇曰 - 'Wěi did not accept it, but instead addressed the snake, saying...' This shows humility and a desire to help rather than receive a reward.
  • “龙王能施云雨，阴阳莫测，神变由心，行藏在己，必能有道，拯拔沉沦。” - 'Lord Dragon King, you can summon clouds and rain, your power is beyond measure, your transformations are from the heart, your actions are within your control. You must surely have the way to rescue those who are lost.' This is a poetic and reverent statement, invoking the divine powers of the Dragon King.
  • “倘赐挈维，得还人世，则死生感激，铭在肺腑，但遂归心，不愿怀宝。” - 'If you would grant me aid and allow me to return to the human world, I would be eternally grateful, and this gratitude would be engraved in my heart. But I only wish to return to my heart’s original place, not to hold onto treasures.' This is a humble and sincere request, expressing a desire for spiritual or physical return rather than material gain.
  • 蛇遂吞珠，蜿蜒将有所适 - 'The snake then swallowed the pearl and, winding its way, seemed to be going somewhere.' This marks the conclusion of the interaction and the departure of the supernatural being.
  • 讳即再拜，跨蛇而出 - 'He (Wěi) then bowed twice and leapt onto the snake, emerging from the cave.' '讳' here may be a surname or title, but it is unclear. '再拜' is a formal act of reverence, and '跨蛇而出' is a literal description of exiting via the snake.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蛇 - In Chinese mythology, snakes are often associated with transformation, wisdom, and the supernatural. They may be seen as deities or spirits, such as the Dragon King (龙王), who rules over water and the underworld.
  • 龙王 - A major figure in Chinese mythology, the Dragon King is a water deity who controls rain, rivers, and the sea. He is often depicted as a powerful and benevolent being.
  • 灸 - A traditional Chinese medical practice involving the burning of mugwort (艾) near the body to stimulate healing. In this context, it is used on a supernatural being, suggesting a mystical or symbolic healing.
  • 疣 - In traditional Chinese medicine and folklore, warts or growths may be seen as signs of illness, curses, or supernatural afflictions.
  • 珠 - Pearls in Chinese mythology are often symbols of wisdom, purity, and divine favor. Receiving a pearl from a supernatural being is a mark of great honor and gratitude.
  • 再拜 - A formal act of respect, often performed before a deity or superior being.
  • 跨蛇而出 - This is a fantastical and symbolic way of exiting a mystical or subterranean realm, suggesting a transformation or rebirth.

Proper Nouns

  • 炜 - Likely a personal name or title. In the text, it is used as the subject of the narrative, so it should be translated as a proper noun.
  • 龙王 - A proper noun and title, referring to the deity of water and the underworld.
  • 讳 - This term is used at the end: '讳即再拜，跨蛇而出.' The term '讳' can mean 'to conceal' or 'to avoid mentioning' and is often used in classical texts to refer to the name of a deceased person. However, in this context, it may be a surname or title, and its meaning is uncertain. It is best to treat it as a proper noun and translate it as 'Hui' or 'Hui (name)' with a note of uncertainty.
