Idiomatic Expressions

  • 去不由穴口 - Literally means 'did not go through the cave entrance,' indicating an unconventional or indirect route.
  • 只于洞中行可数十里 - Means 'only traveled within the cave for dozens of miles,' emphasizing the distance covered inside the cave.
  • 其中幽暗若漆 - Describes the cave as 'dark as ink,' using a vivid simile to convey the darkness.
  • 但蛇之光烛两壁 - Means 'only the snake's light illuminated the two walls,' suggesting the snake's role in providing light.
  • 时见绘画古丈夫，咸有冠带 - Translates to 'occasionally saw paintings of ancient men, all adorned with hats and ribbons,' indicating the presence of historical or mythological figures.
  • 最后触一石门，门有金兽环，洞然明朗 - Means 'finally touched a stone door, which had a golden beast ring and was suddenly bright and clear,' describing the transition from darkness to light.
  • 蛇抵此不进，而卸下炜 - Translates to 'the snake stopped here and let go of Wei,' indicating the snake's action and Wei's position.
  • 炜将谓已达人世矣 - Means 'Wei thought he had reached the human world,' expressing Wei's expectation or belief.
  • 入户，但见一室，空阔可百余步 - Translates to 'entering the room, he saw a spacious space of over a hundred steps,' describing the room's size.
  • 穴之四壁，皆镌为房室 - Means 'the four walls of the cave were carved into rooms,' indicating the intricate design of the cave.
  • 当中有锦绣数间，垂金泥紫帏，更饰以珠玉，炫晃如明星之缀 - Translates to 'in the center were several rooms with brocade, hanging with golden and purple curtains, further adorned with jewels and pearls, shining like stars,' describing the opulence and beauty of the rooms.
  • 帐前有金炉，炉上有蛟龙鸾凤龟蛇燕雀，皆开口喷出香烟，芳芬蓊郁 - Means 'in front of the curtain was a golden incense burner, with dragons, phoenixes, turtles, snakes, swallows, and sparrows on it, all spouting fragrant smoke,' describing the incense burner and its decorations.
  • 旁有小池，砌以金壁，贮以水银 - Translates to 'beside it was a small pond, lined with golden walls, filled with mercury,' describing the pond's construction and contents.
  • 凫之类，皆琢琼瑶而泛之 - Means 'ducks and other birds were carved from jade and floated in the pond,' indicating the decorative elements in the pond.
  • 四壁有床，咸饰以犀象，上有琴瑟笙簧鼗鼓祝，不可胜记 - Translates to 'on the four walls were beds, all adorned with ivory and rhinoceros horn, with musical instruments such as zithers, flutes, and drums, too numerous to count,' describing the beds and their decorations.
  • 炜细视手泽尚新 - Means 'Wei carefully examined and saw that the handprints were still fresh,' indicating the recent use of the room.
  • 乃恍然莫测是何洞府也 - Translates to 'he was suddenly bewildered and could not fathom what kind of cave this was,' expressing Wei's confusion and wonder.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蛇之光烛两壁 - The snake's light is a metaphor for the source of illumination, possibly symbolizing a mystical or supernatural element.
  • 绘画古丈夫，咸有冠带 - The paintings of ancient men with hats and ribbons suggest a connection to historical or mythological figures, possibly indicating a sacred or ceremonial space.
  • 金兽环 - The golden beast ring on the door is a symbol of wealth and status, often found in imperial or noble settings.
  • 锦绣数间，垂金泥紫帏 - The use of brocade, golden and purple curtains, and other luxurious materials indicates a high level of opulence and possibly a royal or divine setting.
  • 蛟龙鸾凤龟蛇燕雀 - These mythical creatures are often associated with imperial or divine symbols, suggesting a sacred or ceremonial space.
  • 金炉 - The golden incense burner is a common feature in temples and imperial palaces, symbolizing purity and sanctity.
  • 水银 - Mercury, often used in alchemical and medicinal practices, suggests a connection to esoteric knowledge or practices.
  • 琼瑶 - Jade and other precious stones are symbols of wealth and purity, often used in decorative arts.
  • 琴瑟笙簧鼗鼓祝 - Musical instruments are often associated with rituals and ceremonies, indicating a sacred or ceremonial space.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '去不由穴口，只于洞中行可数十里' describes the unconventional route taken by the travelers, emphasizing the distance traveled inside the cave.
  • The simile '其中幽暗若漆' creates a vivid image of the cave's darkness.
  • The use of 'but' in '但蛇之光烛两壁' introduces the snake's light as the primary source of illumination, creating a contrast with the darkness.
  • The phrase '时见绘画古丈夫，咸有冠带' introduces the presence of historical or mythological figures.
  • The transition from darkness to light in '最后触一石门，门有金兽环，洞然明朗' is marked by the appearance of the stone door and its golden beast ring.
  • The snake's action of stopping and letting go of Wei in '蛇抵此不进，而卸下炜' creates a moment of tension and curiosity.
  • Wei's expectation of reaching the human world in '炜将谓已达人世矣' sets up the contrast with the actual discovery.
  • The description of the spacious room in '入户，但见一室，空阔可百余步' sets the stage for the subsequent discoveries.
  • The intricate design of the cave walls into rooms in '穴之四壁，皆镌为房室' suggests a highly organized and elaborate space.
  • The opulent and beautiful rooms in '当中有锦绣数间，垂金泥紫帏，更饰以珠玉，炫晃如明星之缀' are described in detail, emphasizing their grandeur.
  • The incense burner and its decorations in '帐前有金炉，炉上有蛟龙鸾凤龟蛇燕雀，皆开口喷出香烟，芳芬蓊郁' are described in detail, emphasizing their sacred or ceremonial nature.
  • The pond's construction and contents in '旁有小池，砌以金壁，贮以水银' are described in detail, suggesting a connection to esoteric knowledge or practices.
  • The decorative elements in the pond in '凫之类，皆琢琼瑶而泛之' are described, emphasizing their beauty and craftsmanship.
  • The beds and their decorations in '四壁有床，咸饰以犀象，上有琴瑟笙簧鼗鼓祝，不可胜记' are described in detail, emphasizing their luxurious and ceremonial nature.
  • Wei's examination of the handprints in '炜细视手泽尚新' indicates recent use, adding a sense of immediacy and realism.
  • Wei's confusion and wonder in '乃恍然莫测是何洞府也' set the tone for the mysterious and awe-inspiring nature of the cave.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 良久 - Classical expression meaning 'for a long time' or 'after a while,' indicating a period of waiting or inaction.
  • 取琴試彈 - Literally 'take up the zither and try to play,' indicating a tentative or experimental action.
  • 四壁戶牖皆啟 - Poetic expression meaning 'all the walls, doors, and windows were open,' suggesting an open or supernatural space.
  • 有小青衣出而笑曰 - A small figure in green clothing came out and said with a smile; '青衣' refers to a person in green clothing, often associated with servants or minor spirits.
  • 玉京子 - A title or name, possibly referring to a deity or a spiritual figure, likely associated with Daoist cosmology. '玉京' is a term for a celestial palace or abode, and '子' is a respectful suffix.
  • 崔家郎 - A respectful term for a man from the Cui family, indicating nobility or social standing.
  • 須臾 - A classical term meaning 'a short while later' or 'soon after.'
  • 古鬟髻 - A description of ancient hairstyles, specifically the '古鬟' or 'ancient bun,' indicating a traditional or mythological appearance.
  • 曳霓裳之衣 - 'Wearing robes of rainbow-like clothing,' a poetic description of ethereal or divine attire.
  • 何崔子擅入皇帝玄宮耶 - 'Why has the Cui gentleman擅自 entered the Emperor's dark palace?' A rhetorical question expressing surprise or accusation.
  • 再拜 - A formal act of bowing twice, a sign of deep respect or submission.
  • 酬拜 - A reciprocal bow, showing mutual respect or acknowledgment.
  • 皇帝玄宮 - 'The Emperor's dark palace,' likely a metaphorical or supernatural term for a sacred or hidden place.
  • 暫赴祝融宴爾 - 'He has temporarily gone to a banquet of Zhurong,' where '祝融' is a mythological fire deity, and '宴' is a banquet or feast.
  • 就榻鼓琴 - 'To sit on the bed and play the zither,' indicating a casual or intimate setting for performance.
  • 《胡笳》 - A classical Chinese musical piece, often associated with the story of Cai Wenji (蔡文姬), a famous female musician and poet of the Han dynasty.
  • 大是新曲 - 'It is indeed a new piece,' expressing surprise or admiration.
  • 酌醴傳觞 - 'Pouring sweet wine and passing the cup,' a poetic description of a drinking ceremony or feast.
  • 叩首求歸 - 'Kneeling and bowing to the ground, requesting to return,' indicating deep supplication or a desire to return to one’s original place.
  • 詞旨颇切 - 'The words and meaning are very sincere or heartfelt,' indicating emotional intensity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 琴 - The Chinese zither, a traditional musical instrument often associated with scholars and immortals.
  • 玉京子 - Likely a Daoist or celestial title. '玉京' refers to the celestial palace of the Jade京, a sacred place in Daoist cosmology. '子' is a respectful suffix, often used for sages or immortals.
  • 崔家郎 - The Cui family was a prominent aristocratic family in Chinese history, known for their status and influence. '郎' is a respectful term for a young man of noble birth.
  • 皇帝玄宮 - A metaphorical or supernatural term for a sacred or hidden palace of the Emperor. It may refer to a Daoist or celestial realm.
  • 祝融 - A mythological fire god and one of the Four Symbols of Chinese cosmology. He is also associated with the southern direction and the element of fire.
  • 《胡笳》 - A famous musical composition attributed to Cai Wenji (蔡文姬), a Han dynasty poet and musician. The piece is based on the story of her capture by the Xiongnu and her eventual return to China.
  • 蔡文姬 - Cai Wenji (also known as Cai Yan), a famous female poet and musician of the Eastern Han dynasty. She is known for her tragic life and the composition of the *Hujia* (胡笳) music.
  • 中郎邕 - Refers to Cai Yong (蔡邕), a famous Han dynasty scholar, musician, and calligrapher, and the father of Cai Wenji.
  • 被虏没于胡中 - 'Was captured and lost in the land of the Hu (barbarians),' referring to Cai Wenji’s capture by the Xiongnu during the chaos of the Han dynasty.
  • 抚琴而成斯弄 - 'Composed this piece by playing the zither,' indicating that the *Hujia* was inspired by her experiences and emotions.
  • 象胡中吹笳哀咽之韵 - 'Imitating the mournful sound of the Hu (barbarian) flute,' referring to the emotional and musical style of the *Hujia* piece.

Proper Nouns

  • 玉京子 - A title or name, possibly referring to a Daoist deity or immortal. Likely a proper noun, though the exact identity is uncertain.
  • 崔家郎 - A term referring to a nobleman from the Cui family. '崔' (Cui) is a proper surname, and '郎' is a respectful honorific.
  • 炜 - A name, likely the protagonist of the story. Should be consistently rendered as 'Wei' in English.
  • 蔡文姬 - A proper name, Cai Wenji, a famous Han dynasty poet and musician.
  • 中郎邕 - A proper name, Cai Yong (蔡邕), a famous scholar and musician of the Han dynasty.
  • 祝融 - A proper name, the mythological fire deity.
  • 《胡笳》 - A proper name of a musical composition, the *Hujia* (also sometimes translated as *Hornpipe* or *Hu Jia*).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 崔子既来 - Indicates that 'Cui Zi has already arrived,' setting the context for the dialogue.

  • 及广其宇 - And expanded its hall. '及' is a conjunction meaning 'and' or 'also'; '广其宇' means 'expand its space' or 'expand its hall.'
  • 是知羊城即广州城隍庙，有五羊焉 - Thus, one knows that Yangcheng is the City God Temple of Guangzhou, which has five sheep. '是知' is a classical way of saying 'thus one knows' or 'therefore it is known that.'
  • 又征任翁之室 - Then he inquired about the room of Mr. Ren. '征' here is used as 'to inquire about' or 'to investigate.'

  • 遂丰洁香撰甘醴 - He then prepared rich, clean incense and sweet liquor. '丰洁' means 'rich and clean'; '香撰' is incense offerings; '甘醴' is sweet wine or liquor.
  • 留于蒲涧寺之僧室 - Left it at the monk’s chamber of Pu Jian Temple. '蒲涧寺' is the name of a temple; '僧室' is the monk’s room or chamber.
  • 夜半，果四女伴田夫人至 - At midnight, indeed, four women accompanied Lady Tian arrived. '果' means 'indeed' or 'actually'; '四女伴' is 'four women companions' or 'four female attendants.'
  • 容仪艳逸，言旨澹雅 - Their appearance was beautiful and elegant, their words gentle and refined. '容仪' is appearance or bearing; '艳逸' is beautiful and elegant; '言旨' is the meaning or intent of the words; '澹雅' is gentle and refined.
  • 四女与崔生会饮谐谑 - The four women met with Cui the young man, drank together, and engaged in witty banter. '谐谑' means 'witty banter' or 'humorous conversation.'

  • 致书达于越王，卑辞厚礼，敬荷而已 - He wrote a letter to the King of Yue, with humble words and rich gifts, and respectfully offered them. '卑辞' is humble words; '厚礼' is rich gifts; '敬荷' is to respectfully bear or present.
  • 遂与夫人归室 - Then he returned with the lady to the chamber. '归室' is to return to the chamber or room.
  • 因诘夫人曰 - He then questioned the lady, saying... '诘' is to question or interrogate.
  • 既是齐王女，何以远配于南越 - If you are the daughter of King Qi, why were you married far away to Nan Yue? '远配' is 'married far away' or 'married to a distant place.'

  • 王薨因以为殉，乃今不知几时也 - When the king died, I was made a殉 (sacrificial victim), and now I do not know how long ago that was. '殉' is a sacrificial victim, often used for concubines or attendants who died with their lord.

  • 惊起寻之 - 'Startled and got up to look for it.' This is a compound action: being startled and then searching.
  • 堂中垂帘帏 - 'In the hall, hanging curtains.' 垂帘 literally means 'hanging down curtains,' often used in classical texts to describe a setting that is secluded or mysterious.
  • 喧然笑语 - 'Noisy laughter and conversation.' 喧然 is an adverb meaning 'noisily' or 'loudly.'
  • 夷、胜突入 - 'Yi and Sheng suddenly entered.' 突入 is a strong verb meaning 'to rush in' or 'to burst in.'
  • 屏帏四合 - 'The screen and curtains all around.' 四合 means 'surrounding all around' or 'encircling.'
  • 奇香扑人 - 'Strange fragrance struck the person.' 扑人 is a vivid expression meaning 'to strike the person' or 'to overwhelm the senses.'
  • 雕盘珍膳 - 'Elaborately carved trays with precious delicacies.' 雕盘 refers to ornate trays, 珍膳 to rare or fine food.
  • 不可名状 - 'Impossible to describe.' A fixed expression meaning 'too extraordinary to be described in words.'
  • 娇丽无比 - 'Incomparably beautiful and charming.' 娇丽 is a compound term meaning 'delicate and beautiful.'
  • 与窦对食 - 'To eat with Dou.' 对食 is a classical term meaning 'to share a meal together' or 'to dine together.'
  • 侍婢十余人，亦皆端妙 - 'More than ten attendants, all of whom were also graceful and beautiful.' 端妙 is a compound term meaning 'graceful and exquisite.'
  • 银炉煮茗方熟 - 'The silver stove was just boiling the tea.' 煮茗 is a classical term for 'boiling tea' or 'brewing tea.'
  • 坐者起入西厢帷中 - 'The seated one rose and entered the western chamber’s curtain.' 西厢 refers to a western pavilion or chamber, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a secluded or romantic setting.
  • 曰：“是何儿郎，冲突人家。” - 'Said, 'What kind of young man is this, intruding into someone’s home?'' 儿郎 is a term for 'young man' or 'youth,' 冲突人家 means 'to intrude into a family's home' or 'to disturb a household.'
  • 面色如土，端坐不语 - 'His face was as pale as clay, sitting upright without speaking.' 面色如土 is an idiom meaning 'pale as clay,' indicating shock or fear.
  • 夷、胜无以致辞，啜茗而出 - 'Yi and Sheng had no words to say, drank tea, and left.' 无以致辞 means 'had no words to speak' or 'could not respond.'
  • 既下阶，闻闭户之声 - 'After descending the steps, they heard the sound of the door closing.' 下阶 means 'to descend the steps' or 'to go down the stairs.'
  • 风狂儿郎，因何共止？ - 'Mad young man, why did you stay here?' 风狂 is an idiom for 'mad' or 'wild,' 共止 means 'to stay together' or 'to stay in the same place.'
  • 古人所以卜邻者，岂虚语哉！ - 'This is why the ancients used to choose neighbors carefully—was this not a true saying?' 卜邻 is a classical term for 'choosing a neighbor' or 'selecting a neighbor,' often with the implication of choosing a good or virtuous person.
  • 窦辞以“非己所居，难拒异客。必虑轻侮，岂无他宅。” - Dou responded with, 'It is not my own residence, so it is hard to refuse a strange guest. I must be concerned about being insulted, so is there not another house?'
  • 因复欢笑 - 'Then they laughed again.' 因复 means 'then again' or 'thus once more.'

  • 夷、胜 - These are names of individuals. 夷 and 胜 are common Chinese surnames or personal names. There is no clear historical reference for these names in the text, so they are likely fictional or from a specific narrative context.
  • 窦 - This is a surname, likely referring to a character named Dou. In classical Chinese literature, 窦 is a common surname and may be linked to historical or literary figures (e.g., the historical figure Dong Zhuo or Dou Xian), but in this context, it is more likely a fictional character.
  • 西厢 - A term commonly used in classical Chinese literature to refer to a secluded or romantic chamber, often in the context of a pavilion or small hall in a courtyard. It is famously used in the classical novel The Western Chamber (西厢记), a tale of romance and courtship.
  • 卜邻 - The phrase 卜邻 refers to the ancient Chinese practice of carefully choosing one's neighbors, often based on virtue or social standing. It appears in classical texts and is a metaphor for social responsibility and the importance of associating with the right people.
  • 对食 - A term used in classical Chinese to describe sharing a meal together, often with connotations of intimacy or social interaction. It can also be used in a more specific sense in certain historical contexts.
  • 银炉煮茗 - Refers to the act of boiling tea in a silver stove. Tea-drinking was a refined cultural practice in classical China, and the use of silver stoves indicates a luxurious or aristocratic setting.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The text is written in 文言文 (Classical Chinese), reflecting the literary style of ancient China. It alludes to the social structure of ancient China, where people often moved due to various reasons such as marriage or relocation. The passage also includes references to ancient Chinese etiquette, such as formal greetings and polite language.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '又五年，再赴调，亟寻旧游，茫无所睹矣' describes a series of events: after five years, the person is summoned again, seeks out old friends, but finds them nowhere to be seen. The ambiguity lies in the temporal sequence and the emotional tone. The sentence '怅然空还，忽遇之于半途' describes a sudden encounter after a disappointing return. The ambiguity lies in the emotional shift from disappointment to surprise. The sentence '虽年貌加长，而容态益媚秀' describes a change in appearance over time. The ambiguity lies in the contrast between aging and becoming more graceful. The sentence '即呼揖问讯' describes a formal greeting. The ambiguity lies in the politeness and formality of the interaction. The sentence '隔阔滋久，君已忘之耶' describes a long separation and a question about whether the person has forgotten. The ambiguity lies in the emotional tone and the implied nostalgia.

[1209]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西施若解亡吴国，越国亡来又是谁 - Rhetorical question playing on the legend of Xi Shi; '若解' means 'if she understood,' and '亡吴国' means 'the fall of Wu.' The second line is a pun on Xi Shi's name.
  • 大都才人，四山五岳，精灵间气之所聚会 - Poetic description of talented individuals as the essence of the mountains and rivers; '大都' means 'mostly' or 'generally,' and '精灵间气之所聚会' means 'the gathering place of spirits and the finest energies.'
  • 有何新声，倾耳一听 - Rhetorical question asking what new sounds or ideas are worth listening to; '有何新声' means 'what new sounds,' and '倾耳一听' means 'to listen intently.'
  • 家是红罗亭上仙，来尘世已多年 - Poetic description of a woman as a celestial being from the Red Silk Pavilion; '家是红罗亭上仙' means 'her home is the Red Silk Pavilion, where she is a celestial being,' and '来尘世已多年' means 'she has come to the mortal world for many years.'
  • 君心既逐东流水，错把无缘当有缘 - Metaphorical expression suggesting a fleeting or unattainable love; '君心既逐东流水' means 'your heart follows the east-flowing river,' and '错把无缘当有缘' means 'mistakenly taking what is not meant to be as something meant to be.'
  • 掀篷揽衣，跃入水中 - Vivid description of the woman jumping into the water; '掀篷揽衣' means 'lifting the canopy and taking her clothes,' and '跃入水中' means 'jumping into the water.'
  • 汗背而觉，一梦境也 - Description of the man waking up from a dream; '汗背而觉' means 'feeling sweat on his back and waking up,' and '一梦境也' means 'it was just a dream.'
  • 翌日，事传吴下 - Statement that the next day, the story spread throughout Wu; '翌日' means 'the next day,' and '事传吴下' means 'the story spread throughout Wu.'
